coredata.dc:title: Scandium detection of carbon dot based on bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:issueName: Photoluminescence in Rare Earths: Photonic Materials and Devices (PRE'24)
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Zhe
Li, Heyu
Pittman, Aaron S.
Cao, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Scandium is a rare earth element that is relatively scarce on Earth. The need for its rapid qualitative detection has increased dramatically owing to its extensive use in various high-tech sectors and potential environmental concerns. This work aims to develop a portable, simple, low-cost specific detection sensor for on-site qualitative detection of Sc3+ ions. Multiple carbon dots were synthesized via the “bottom-up” hydrothermal method and characterized by morphology, structure, and optical properties. The characteristic structure of “salicylic acid” was determined as the core of the Sc3+ selective detection function through precursor molecular engineering design. Further investigation was conducted into the impact of various factors on the Sc3+ specific fluorescence detection performance of related derived carbon dots, including the electronic and spatial effects of various substituents on the aromatic skeleton of precursor molecules and the size of aromatic conjugated systems. The detection performance of carbon dots derived from different precursor sources was compared, and the optimal carbon dots derived from 2-hydroxy-3-naphthoic acid were selected, with a lower detection limit (4.38 ppb) and a significant blue shift (from green visible light to blue visible light range). On this basis, a portable device capable of detecting Sc3+ ions, the 2-3-CDs PVA membrane, was developed. Our work presents the first discovery of carbon dots as Sc3+ ion fluorescence sensors, providing new insights and beneficial results for subsequent rare earth element detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Scandium
Specific detection
Fluorescence analysis

Anti-interference detection of Sc3+ using o-CDs
Detection of Sc3+ in real samples
Fluorescence characteristics and specific Sc3+ detection of carbon dots
Anti-interference performance of Sc3+ detection by o-CDs
Group space and electronic effects in precursors
Fluorescence characteristics and specific Sc3+ detection of 2-3-CDs
Anti-interference performance of Sc3+ detection by 2-3-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images, (b) particle size distributions of different carbon dots, (1) CDs-OH, (2) CDs-COOH, (3) o-CDs, (4) m-CDs, (5) p-CDs, and (c) FT-IR spectra.
Fluorescence spectra of o-CDs prepared at different (a) raw material concentrations, (b) reaction temperatures, and (c) reaction times.
(a–e) The fluorescence emission spectra and (f) fluorescence intensity changes of five carbon dots.
(a) Fluorescence intensity and fluorescence emission spectra of (b) CDs-OH, (c) CDs-COOH, (d) o-CDs, (e) m-CDs, (f) p-CDs for the detection of rare earth ions.
Linear relationship between Sc3+ concentration and fluorescence intensity of CDs-OH, CDs-COOH, o-CDs, m-CDs, p-CDs.
(a) Fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum, (b) emission spectrum of 2-3-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (c) fluorescence intensity of 2-3-CDs as a function of time.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of rare earth ions detected by 2-3-CDs (b) comparison of fluorescence intensity(F/F0) of rare earth ions detected by 2-3-CDs.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of 2-3-CDs spiked with different concentrations of Sc3+ (b) linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of 2-3-CDs and the concentration of Sc3+.

Fluorescence emission spectra of 2-3-CDs-Sc3+ mixed solutions (Blank) with (a) different interfering ions, (b) all rare-earth ions, (c) the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F 0), and (d) quenching efficiency of different rare-earth ions and all rare-earth ions added to 2-3-CDs-Sc3+ mixed solutions.

Fluorescence emission spectra of Sc3+ in the configuration with different Sc3+ concentrations of (a) tap water and (b) lake water, standard curve for the detection of Sc3+ in (c) tap water and (d) lake water by 2-3-CDs.
(a) 2-3-CDs-PVA laminate film under UV light irradiation, (b) Drop of different rare earth ions on the surface of 2-3-CDs-PVA laminate film under UV light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Scandium detection of carbon dot based on bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Chemistry for Inorganic/Organic Hybrid Functionalized Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scandium is a rare earth element that is relatively scarce on Earth. The need for its rapid qualitative detection has increased dramatically owing to its extensive use in various high-tech sectors and potential environmental concerns. This work aims to develop a portable, simple, low-cost specific detection sensor for on-site qualitative detection of Sc3+ ions. Multiple carbon dots were synthesized via the “bottom-up” hydrothermal method and characterized by morphology, structure, and optical properties. The characteristic structure of “salicylic acid” was determined as the core of the Sc3+ selective detection function through precursor molecular engineering design. Further investigation was conducted into the impact of various factors on the Sc3+ specific fluorescence detection performance of related derived carbon dots, including the electronic and spatial effects of various substituents on the aromatic skeleton of precursor molecules and the size of aromatic conjugated systems. The detection performance of carbon dots derived from different precursor sources was compared, and the optimal carbon dots derived from 2-hydroxy-3-naphthoic acid were selected, with a lower detection limit (4.38 ppb) and a significant blue shift (from green visible light to blue visible light range). On this basis, a portable device capable of detecting Sc3+ ions, the 2-3-CDs PVA membrane, was developed. Our work presents the first discovery of carbon dots as Sc3+ ion fluorescence sensors, providing new insights and beneficial results for subsequent rare earth element detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare earth elements (REEs) are known as “industrial vitamins” and are indispensable raw materials in the most advanced scientific and military industries [1–4]. Among them, scandium (Sc) is a highly valuable rare earth element, and its compounds have wide applications in fields such as optics, lasers, aerospace, electronics, catalysis, fuel cells, energy conservation, metallurgy, and medicine [5,6], which has given rise to an increasing demand. In addition to typical rare earth ores, bulk industrial solid wastes (such as fly ash, coal gangue, red mud, etc.) also contain some REEs [7,8]. Usually, the content of REEs in these industrial wastes is only tens to hundreds of ppm, and many elements also interfere with detection, which to some extent causes difficulties in the classification and resource recovery of industrial solid waste. In addition, with the widespread use of various high-tech products related to rare earths and the unreasonable and non-standard development of REE resources, environmental pollution and human health problems related to rare earths are becoming increasingly severe [9–12]. Sc has been adopted as a marker for monitoring environmental matrices (such as plants, water sources, etc.) pollution caused by radionuclides [13]. Therefore, to promote the recovery of REE resources and monitor the content of trace Sc ions (Sc3+) in water, it is urgent to develop sensitive, selective, fast, and low-cost analysis methods for low-concentration REEs based on complex systems [14].

Up to now, the main analytical techniques used for REE detection include X-ray fluorescence (XRF), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), instrumental neutron activation analysis (INAA), inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), fluorescence analysis, etc. [15–17]. Although ICP technology has been widely used in qualitative and quantitative analysis due to its high precision and accuracy [18], its cumbersome and expensive equipment and the time-consuming sample preparation process have limited its application scenarios for rapid on-site analysis [19]. Fluorescence photometry has advantages such as simple sample preparation, fast detection, and low instrument cost, making it very suitable for portable monitoring devices deployed on-site. Therefore, rare earth detection methods based on fluorescence photometry have received increasing attention [20].

Carbon dots are a novel semiconductor carbon-based nano luminescent material with a scale of less than 10 nm [21]. Due to the excellent photoluminescence properties of carbon dots, they are commonly used as fluorescent probes for chemical sensing and biological imaging [22–25]. Based on the unique structural characteristics of carbon dots, it may be possible to achieve selective recognition of low-concentration rare earth ions in complex systems by forming rare earth complexes on the surface of carbon dots, which has substantial practical significance for the resource recovery and environmental detection of bulk industrial solid waste [26,27].

Developing high-performance fluorescent carbon dot materials for the selective detection of rare earth ions in complex environments is of significant research implication in response to the difficulty in utilizing low-grade rare earth resources. Folic acid was used [28] as a carbon and nitrogen source; by employing the “bottom-up” hydrothermal method, the synthesized carbon dots had a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 85 % and successfully achieved qualitative differentiation of rare earth ions. Nevertheless, the inability to detect rare earth ions precisely remains a challenge. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared by the one-step chemical oxidation method using graphene [29] and anthracite [30] as carbon sources can achieve specific detection of Tb3+, while the carbon dots prepared by optimizing anthracite as the carbon source [31] can also serve as sensitive and selective chemical sensors for Eu3+, with a detection limit of 0.79 μM. Silicon-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs) synthesized using rice husks [32] as carbon sources using carbonization and acid-cutting methods can simultaneously achieve specific detection of Tb3+, Eu3+, and Dy3+. The specific detection range and limit for Tb3+ were 1–12 μM and 3.24 nM, for Eu3+ were 1–16 μM and 0.15 μM, for Dy3+ were 4–30 μM and 2.43 μM. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were prepared using graphene as the carbon source and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) as the nitrogen source [33]. These carbon dots exhibited significant Ce4+ selective coordination and can serve as sensitive and selective chemical sensors for Ce4+, with a detection range and limit of 1–44 mM and 0.8352 mM, respectively.

In order to further promote the development of rare earth ion selective detection technology, this work focused on the specific detection of rare earth element scandium, which has high economic value and wide application. By exploiting the advantages of the “bottom-up” method in the precise design of precursor molecular structures and directional synthesis of functional carbon dots, a series of high-performance fluorescent carbon dot materials containing oxygen-rich groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups were developed based on the previous work experience of our group. Meanwhile, the design rules of different functional groups and specific spatial relationships on precursors in carbon dot synthesis were explored, aiming to achieve low concentration and high selectivity detection of Sc3+. On this basis, a Sc3+ ion-specific fluorescence detection thin film sensor was further developed using optimal carbon dot materials, thus promoting the essential theoretical and application development of fluorescent carbon dots in rare earth ion selective detection, which provides new insights and beneficial results for subsequent rare earth element detection.

Various carbon dots were prepared using different carbon sources as precursors via the “bottom-up” method. The carbon dots derived from different precursors and their abbreviations are shown in Table 1 . For example, 0.1 g of salicylic acid was added to 100 mL of deionized water and stirred vigorously to form a uniform solution. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a PTFE-lined stainless steel high-pressure vessel. The sealed, high-pressure vessel was heated in an oven at 140 °C for 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove components larger than 220 nm. Finally, it was dialyzed against deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm) for 24 h using a 1000 Da dialysis bag, and a clear and transparent carbon dot aqueous solution was obtained.

The rare earth ion solutions were prepared using analytical grade nitrates, and all chemical reagents used in this work were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Unless otherwise specified, solutions were prepared using deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of rare earth ions
ce:section.ce:para: Taking the detection of Sc3+ using salicylic acid-derived carbon dots (o-CDs) as an example, 200 μL of the prepared o-CDs solution was diluted to 50 mL and mixed thoroughly. During the test, 3 mL diluted solution and 15 μL Sc3+ aqueous solution (0.02 mol/L) were taken and mixed in a quartz colorimetric dish for 1 min. Fluorescence detection was performed at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The same procedure and fluorescence detection conditions were also performed for other rare earth ions, including Sc3+, Y3+, La3+, Ce3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+ and Lu3+. Other carbon dots were all tested directly using the original solution without dilution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear relationship test between fluorescence intensity and Sc3+ concentration
ce:section.ce:para:
Sc3+ aqueous solutions with different concentrations were prepared, then 10 μL Sc3+ solution was mixed with carbon dot solution for 1 min, and the fluorescence intensity at the fluorescence emission peak was recorded to investigate its linearity. The detection limit of Sc3+ in carbon dot detection was calculated using the following equation:
LOD =
3 σ
S
Where σ is the standard deviation (n = 11), and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield calculation of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield was calculated using salicylic acid-derived carbon dots (o-CDs) and 2-hydroxy-3-naphthalene-derived carbon dots (2-3-CDs) as an example, and the calculation process is indicated below:
Q = ×
I S
I R
×
A R
A S
×
η s 2
η R 2
Q represents the quantum yield (Quinine sulfate was used as the reference), Q
R represents the quantum of the reference, I
S and I
R represents the integrated fluorescence intensity of samples and reference, A
R and A
S is the absorbance of the samples and the reference,
η s 2
and
η R 2
is the refractive index of the samples and the reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference detection of Sc3+ using o-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Taking Y3+ as an example, 200 μL of the prepared o-CDs solution was diluted to 50 mL and mixed thoroughly. During the test, 3 mL diluted solution and 15 μL Sc3+ aqueous solution (0.02 mol/L) were taken and mixed for 1 min. Then, 15 μL of Y3+ aqueous solution (0.02 mol/L) was added to the o-CDs Sc3+ system and mixed in a quartz colorimetric dish. Fluorescence detection was performed at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The same procedure and fluorescence detection conditions were also performed for other interfering ions, including Y3+, La3+, Ce3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+, and REEs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Sc3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real samples were taken from tap water and a scenic lake in Guangzhou, China. Before use, particulate impurities in the real samples were removed through centrifugation and filtration. Then, the Sc3+ solution with a concentration of 0.003 mol/L to 0.015 mol/L was prepared using tap water and lake water as solvents. 200 μL of o-CDs solution was diluted to 50 mL and mixed evenly. 10 μL of tap water/lake water solution containing Sc3+ was mixed with 3 mL of the previously diluted carbon dot solution in a quartz colorimetric dish for 1 min. Then, the fluorescence detection was performed at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

The size and morphology of the prepared carbon dots were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F microscope). Carbon dots were sonicated and dispersed in ethanol. Preparing TEM samples involved adding two drops of carbon dot ethanol solution onto an ultra-thin carbon film and then drying overnight at room temperature. The functional groups of carbon dots were tested by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet IS 50). The preparation steps of the test sample were as follows: the carbon dot solution was mixed with KBr, placed in an oven for drying, ground evenly in a mortar, and then pressed into pieces for testing, with a testing range of 500–4000 cm−1. The Raman spectrum and thermal stability of carbon dots were characterized by Laser Confocal Raman Spectrometer (LCRS, LabRAM HR800) and Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA, TA SDT650), respectively. The fluorescence spectrum of carbon dots was measured using a quartz colorimetric dish on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer LS-55). The slit for excitation and emission was both 10 nm.

Figxq 1(a–b) shows transmission electron microscopy images and size distribution of CDs-OH, CDs-COOH, o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs, respectively, and all five carbon dots exhibit spherical shapes. The size distribution of CDs OH, CDs COOH, o-CDs, and p-CDs is uniform, and the size of m-CDs was too small, resulting in larger m-CDs being more prominent in the microscopy images. After calculation, the average particle sizes of CDs-OH, CDs-COOH, o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs were 3.61, 5.07, 3.32, 0.92, and 1.59 nm, respectively. The graphite layer of the prepared o-CDs (which exhibited better performance in the following study) can be observed from Fig. S1 in the supplementary information, confirming the successful synthesis of carbon quantum dots.

The characteristic functional groups of the prepared five carbon dots were further identified by FTIR. Fig. 1 (c) shows the infrared spectra of CDs-OH, CDs-COOH, o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs. The prepared five carbon dots exhibited corresponding O-H stretching vibration, C-H stretching, and symmetric vibration, as well as C=O bending vibration peaks at 3432-3442 cm−1, 2918-2925 cm−1, 2850-2854 cm−1 and 1627-1633 cm−1, respectively [34–38]. The peaks at 1378-1406 cm−1 and 1052-1099 cm−1 corresponded to C-OH and C-O-C functional groups, respectively. Meanwhile, C-C functional groups were also found at 1467 cm−1 for CDs-OH and CDs-COOH, while C-O functional groups were found at 1248 cm−1 for o-CDs. From the FTIR results, all five carbon dots demonstrated the oxygen-containing functional groups, including carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups, which enhance the hydrophilicity of the carbon dots and provide readily accessible binding sites for rare earth ions, achieving the goal of detecting rare earth ions.

We observe from the infrared spectra that o-CDs have the highest content of various functional groups, indicating that in constructing carbon dots using the “bottom-up” method, the dehydration polymerization and crosslinking reactions of o-CDs were the most complete. The infrared results also indicated that when hydroxyl and carboxyl groups were adjacent to the benzene ring, small molecules had the lowest resistance and a complete reaction during dehydration polymerization and carbonization growth, resulting in the highest content of carbon dot functional groups. In addition, the Raman spectroscopy was used to investigate the surface structure of CDs, thereby facilitating the comprehension of the amorphous and crystalline characteristics of the synthesized CDs. As shown in Fig. S2 of the supplementary information, the intensity ratio ID/IG for both CDs was less than 2, indicating the crystallinity of the synthesized CDs and suggesting an elevated degree of graphitization [39]. To further enhance comprehension of the bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted (Fig. S3). The results indicated that the tested carbon dots all exhibit three stages of weight. Stage 1 was within the range of 45–50 °C, with a weight loss of 1.6–3.77 %, stage 2 was the main weight loss stage, with a mass loss of 82.25–93.46 %, and stage 3 indicated a weight loss of 3.67 % above 250 °C, resulting in a residue percentage of about 8.76 %. The weight loss of both carbon dots may result from the evaporation of water molecules linked to the surface of the CDs and a progressive loss of oxygen-containing groups, including CH. It was worth noting that the temperature range where carbon dots experienced significant weight loss was much higher than the actual application temperature of carbon dots. The carbon dots were only applied to the specific detection of scandium in an aqueous solution at ambient temperatures.

Under the irradiation of a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of o-CDs was the highest. Therefore, o-CDs were used as a representative to explore the optimal reaction conditions, including the influence of raw material concentration, reaction temperature, and reaction time during the preparation process on the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots.

By comparing the fluorescence spectra of carbon dots prepared under different conditions, as shown in Fig. 2 , the optimal preparation conditions for o-CDs can be obtained: raw material concentration of 1 mg/mL, reaction time of 6 h, and reaction temperature of 140 °C. Various carbon dots derived from other precursors were prepared under the same optimal preparation conditions to control the variables of subsequent experiments and guarantee that difference only lay in the precursor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characteristics and specific Sc3+ detection of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence characteristics of five prepared carbon dots were studied using fluorescence spectroscopy, and their corresponding fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were shown in Fig. S4 in the supplementary information. The optimal excitation wavelength for CDs-OH was 300 nm, CDs-COOH was 300 nm, o-CDs was 294 nm, m-CDs was 287 nm, and p-CDs was 252 nm.

Fig. 3 (a–e) shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-OH, CDs-COOH, o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. The five-carbon dot samples exhibited similar phenomena, and the fluorescence intensity of the prepared carbon dot solution changed dramatically with the change of excitation wavelength, while the position of the emission peak remained unchanged. Because of the weak fluorescence intensity, the emission peak shifted at excitation wavelengths of 320, 330, and 340 nm. Further adjustment of the carbon dot concentration to enhance its fluorescence intensity revealed that the emission peak position was still around 410 nm, revealing that the emission wavelengths of the five prepared carbon dots were independent of excitation, which was different from most conventional carbon dots. We surmised this may be due to the prepared carbon dots' relatively uniform surface state, and this emission wavelength-independent behavior can provide reliable stability for carbon dots as fluorescence sensors. Fig. 3(f) shows the changes in fluorescence intensity of CDs-OH, CDs-COOH, o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs over time. The stability of these fluorescence sensors was observed every 5 min at an emission wavelength of 410 nm. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots tended to stabilize with time, indicating excellent fluorescence stability of these carbon dots.

The fluorescence intensity of five prepared carbon dot (CD) solutions and their corresponding CD-Sc3+ were tested at different excitation wavelengths to determine the optimal detection wavelength for Sc3+, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). F
0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak corresponding to the CD solution, and F represents the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak corresponding to CD-Sc3+. It can be observed that the fluorescence enhancement amplitude of all CD solutions was the highest when the excitation wavelength was 320 nm. Therefore, 320 nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength in the subsequent detection of rare earth ions. Fig. 4(b–f) shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs-OH, CDs-COOH, o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs with the addition of various rare earth ions. After adding Sc3+ to these five CD solutions, the fluorescence emission peak intensity increased, and the peak position shifted to blue. Meanwhile, the intensity and peak position remained unchanged after adding other rare earth ions.

As shown in Fig. 4 and Table 2 , the fluorescence emission peaks of these five carbon dots were 410 nm, 420 nm, 410 nm, 415 nm, and 410 nm, respectively. After adding Sc3+, their fluorescence emission peaks blue-shifted to 390 nm, 400 nm, 390 nm, 390 nm, and 390 nm, and the fluorescence intensity increased by 3.24, 1.76, 4.23, 2.36, and 2.10 times, respectively. Furthermore, the quantum yield of the most promising o-CDs sample was calculated, and quinine sulfate was used as the reference. The integrated fluorescence intensity of the sample and reference was obtained from the peak area shown in Fig. S5 in the supplementary information, and the quantum yield of o-CDs was 4.98 %. After the addition of other rare earth ions, the fluorescence intensity of these five CD solutions fluctuated around 1.0, indicating that carbon dots prepared with phenol, benzoic acid, salicylic acid, m-hydroxybenzoic acid, and p-hydroxybenzoic acid as carbon sources showed high Sc3+ selectivity when detecting rare earth ions, revealing the practical application potential of these five carbon dots as Sc3+ fluorescence sensors.

Fig. 5 shows the functional relationship between Sc3+ concentration and relative fluorescence intensity (F/F
0 ) of carbon dots. Their linear regression equations and R2 results are also shown in Fig. 5. Among all the calibration curves, the slope of o-CDs was the highest, which meant that the fluorescence intensity of o-CDs responded the fastest to changes in Sc3+ ion concentration and had the highest sensitivity. The calculated limits of detection (LOD) for the five carbon dots (CDs-OH, CDs-COOH, o-CDs, m-CDs, p-CDs) were 0.453 μM (20.4 ppb), 2.511 μM (112.9 ppb), 0.229 μM (10.3 ppb), 2.464 μM (110.8 ppb), and 3.961 μM (178.1 ppb), respectively. The o-CDs with the lowest detection limit indicated their greatest practical application potential; thus, o-CDs were chosen for further testing of actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference performance of Sc3+ detection by o-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to study the anti-interference properties of o-CDs on Sc3+ specific detection, interference ions (Y3+, La3+, Ce3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+) were added to the solution of o-CDs in the presence of Sc3+ (i.e., Blank referred to the mixture of o-CDs solution with only Sc3+ added). Fig. 6 (a) shows the fluorescence emission spectrum of o-CDs solution, o-CDs solution with only Sc3+ added (Blank), and the subsequent addition of various interfering ions. The change in fluorescence emission peak was minimal, implying the weak influence of other rare earth ions as interfering ions on Sc3+. Subsequently, o-CDs were used as a Sc3+specific fluorescence sensor to examine their anti-interference efficacy against all other rare earth ions, as illustrated in Fig. 6(b). The addition of all rare earth ions slightly quenched the Sc3+ fluorescence emission intensity of o-CDs without changing the position of the fluorescence peak. Fig. 6(c) shows a columnar comparison of the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F
0 ) of o-CDs solution with only Sc3+ added (Blank) and with the addition of various individual interfering ions and all rare earth ions. The results showed that o-CDs solution with only Sc3+ added (Blank) can increase the fluorescence intensity of o-CDs by 4.25 times, while adding interfering ions resulted in minimal variation in relative fluorescence intensity, ranging from 3.94 to 4.47. It is worth noting that when detecting all rare earth ions, the relative fluorescence intensity decreased from 4.25 when only Sc3+ was detected to 3.69. These results demonstrated the excellent anti-interference performance of o-CDs as Sc3+ specific fluorescence sensors.

The performance of the prepared o-CDs in real samples (tap water and lake water samples) was further investigated to evaluate the potential application of o-CDs as Sc3+ fluorescence sensors. Fig. 6(d) and (e) present the standard curves for determining Sc3+, and the linear correlation can be observed within the range of 10–50 μM for both tap water and lake water. It was worth noting that, unlike Fig. 5 (where the detection limit range for various carbon dots was 0–30 μM), the linearity of the X-axis was poor when utilizing the actual sample concentration (Sc3+) with a test range of 10–50 μM, as shown in Fig. S6 in the supplementary information. Therefore, the concentration (Sc3+) on the X-axis was represented logarithmically, subsequently allowing for the calculation of the linear equation of the actual sample, as illustrated in Fig. 6. The linear equation for tap water was F/F
0 = 0.7649lnCSc(Ш)-0.355, with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9958, and the linear equation for lake water was F/F
0 = 0.8093lnCSc(Ш)-0.4041, with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9958. The above tests have demonstrated the excellent detection performance of this o-CDs fluorescent sensor, even in the presence of various metal ions and interfering substances in real samples, which further manifested the broad prospects of o-CDs in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Group space and electronic effects in precursors
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots prepared using phenol (CDs-OH) and benzoic acid (CDs-COOH) as precursors can achieve specific detection, thus determining the key roles of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in constructing such carbon dots for specific detection of Sc3+. Subsequently, by using o-hydroxybenzoic acid (salicylic acid, o-CDs), m-hydroxybenzoic acid (m-CDs), and p-hydroxybenzoic acid (p-CDs) as carbon sources and adjusting the relative positions of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the benzene ring, carbon dots prepared from these three carbon sources can also achieve specific detection. Therefore, it can be determined that the positions of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the precursor do not affect the specific detection of Sc3+ in the constructed carbon dots. Nevertheless, by comparing the performance of the above five carbon dot structures and specific detection of Sc3+, it can be found that the o-CDs with the highest number of surface oxygen-containing functional groups present the highest sensitivity and lowest detection limit for specific detection of Sc3+ [21]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the prepared carbon dots can achieve better performance in the specific detection of Sc3+ by placing hydroxyl and carboxyl groups adjacent to the benzene ring. Based on this, salicylic acid was used as the core structure to explore the mechanism of achieving Sc3+-specific detection by designing and comparing the performance of carbon dots with different precursor structures.

The scenario where methyl groups occupied vacancies on the benzene ring to control the formation of conjugated systems through steric hindrance effects was investigated. The fluorescence emission spectra of rare earth ions detected by these four carbon dots (o-CDs-3-Me, o-CDs-4-Me, o-CDs-5-Me, and o-CDs-6-Me) were shown in Fig. S7, and all of which can achieve the specific detection of Sc3+. Fig. 7 shows the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F/F
0 ) of carbon dots before and after Sc3+ detection. The fluorescence of these four carbon dots increased by 1.79, 4.37, 1.37, and 1.62 times, respectively. Compared with the best detection performance of o-CDs mentioned above, only o-CDs-4-Me had the same performance as o-CDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that during the precursor hydrolysis polymerization, crosslinking, and carbonization process, only the hydrogen atom in the carboxyl group position on the benzene ring did not participate in the reaction. In contrast, the hydrogen atoms in the other positions participated in the reaction. Consequently, once the methyl group occupies the remaining three hydrogen atom sites on the benzene ring, it will not be conducive to forming the structure with the best specific Sc3+ detection performance, resulting in the loss of carbon dot performance.

Further investigation was conducted on the influence of electronic effects of precursors on the formation of carbon dot-conjugated systems. The electron-donating groups (methoxy, methyl, ethyl, thiol) and electron-withdrawing groups (carboxyl, nitro, fluorine, chlorine, bromine) used for substitution of hydroxyl groups in ortho hydroxybenzoic acid were compared and analyzed.

When the methoxy group was chosen as the electron donating group to replace the hydroxyl group of ortho hydroxybenzoic acid, the fluorescence emission spectra of rare earth ions detected by the prepared 2-CDs, 3-CDs, and 4-CDs were shown in Fig. S8, all of which can achieve specific detection of Sc3+. Compared with o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of these three carbon dots increased by 4.45, 4.55, and 3.74 times, respectively. Owing to the similar function of the methoxy group to the hydroxyl group (which can also react with the carboxyl group), the excellent performance of the designed carbon dots in specific detection of Sc3+ was maintained. Moreover, the performance was comparable to that of o-CDs when the methoxy group was located in the ortho and meta positions of the carboxyl group. By comparing methoxy and hydroxyl groups horizontally, carbon dots constructed from methoxy and carboxyl groups have more binding sites with Sc3+, thereby achieving better specific Sc3+ detection performance [25]. Nevertheless, it was worth noting that as an equally important indicator, the fluorescence intensity of these three carbon dots constructed by methoxy and carboxyl groups was far less than that of o-CDs.

The Sc3+ specific detection performance of carbon dots (CDs-CH3, CDs-CH3CH2, and CDs-SH) designed with other electron-donating groups was shown in Fig. 7 to analyze their effects on the formation of carbon dot conjugated systems. The specific detection performance of these three carbon dots for Sc3+ was still retained, and the fluorescence intensity of these three carbon dots increased by 1.25, 1.51, and 1.40 times, respectively. Moreover, CDs-SH showed selectivity for Sc3+ and Tb3+, with a characteristic peak at 546 nm, as shown in Fig. S9. The detection performances were all significantly decreased compared with o-CDs; thus, it can be inferred that in the preparation process of Sc3+ specific detection carbon dot, intramolecular hydrogen bonds between hydroxyl and carboxyl groups promoted the reaction process of carbon dots. The degree of matching between precursor electron-withdrawing and electron-donating groups and the difficulty of the reaction will affect the formation of carbon dots, thereby affecting their performance.

The hydroxyl group was replaced with the selected electron-withdrawing groups, and the performance changes of the synthesized carbon dots were compared. The fluorescence emission spectra of rare earth ions detected by CDs-2COOH, CDs-NO2, CDs-F, CDs-Cl, and CDs-Br are shown in Fig. S10. It can be seen that the five carbon dots can achieve specific detection of Sc3+. The fluorescence intensity of these five carbon dots increased by 1.12, 1.18, 2.39, 1.30, and 2.52 times, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7. Compared with o-CDs, the detection performance of these five carbon dots was significantly reduced. Therefore, it can be concluded that the precursor's electronic effect impacts the specific detection performance of Sc3+. No intramolecular reaction occurred when the same electron withdrawing group was present on the current precursor, affecting the aggregation of the carbon dots. Among the five carbon dots, CDs-F and CDs-Br exhibited more unique characteristics, and it was speculated that in addition to electronic effects, introducing heteroatoms may also impact the formation of carbon dots.

Considering the direct relationship between the fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots and the existence of sp2 hybridized domains originating from the benzene ring in the precursor, and the fact that carbon dots can exhibit better specific Sc3+ detection performance when the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the current precursor were adjacent to the benzene ring, an attempt was made to increase the benzene ring in the precursor thus further optimize the design of carbon dots by selecting naphthoic acid. Based on the fluorescence emission spectra of rare earth ions detected by these three carbon dots, as shown in Fig. S11, only 2–3 CDs could specifically detect Sc3+. The fluorescence emission peak shifted blue from 510 nm to 470 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of 2-3-CDs increased by 5.91 times after detecting Sc3+. Compared with o-CDs, the Sc3+ detection performance of 2-3-CDs was improved, indicating that increasing the aromatic conjugated system of precursors can enhance the specific detection performance of carbon dots for Sc3+, but the position requirements of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the benzene ring were more stringent. Only the structure of 2-hydroxy-3-naphthoic acid explored (2-3-CDs) can improve the detection performance, while carbon dots prepared with other structures lost their specific detection performance for Sc3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characteristics and specific Sc3+ detection of 2-3-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Further investigations were carried out on the optical properties of the optimized 2-3-CDs using fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 8 (a) shows that the optimal excitation wavelength for 2-3-CDs was 350 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength was 510 nm. Moreover, at different excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence emission peaks of 2-3-CDs were mostly located near 510 nm, indicating that the emission wavelength of 2-3-CDs was independent of excitation, which may be due to the relatively uniform and uniform surface state of the prepared 2-3-CDs, which enhanced their excellent stability as rare earth ion fluorescence sensors. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 8(c), the fluorescence intensity of 2-3-CDs remained unchanged over time. The optical properties testing of 2-3-CDs demonstrated their enormous potential as fluorescence sensors.

Fig. 9 shows the fluorescence spectra and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F
0 ) of 2-3-CDs with the addition of various rare earth ions. After adding Sc3+, the fluorescence emission peak of 2-3-CDs shifted from the green visible light to the blue range. This significant blue shift provides a good foundation for the subsequent preparation of 2-3-CDs PVA membranes. 2-3-CDs have high selectivity for Sc3+, and its fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced by 5.90 times, while the addition of other rare earth elements has little effect on the fluorescence intensity change of 2-3-CDs. The above results indicated that the carbon dots prepared with 2-hydroxy-3-naphthoic acid have a significant blue shift from 510 nm to 470 nm, which has fluctuated from green luminescence to blue luminescence, revealing its excellent practical application potential in specific Sc3+ detection.

Fig. 10 (a) shows the fluorescence emission spectra of 2-3-CDs with different Sc3+ concentrations. As the Sc3+ ions concentration increased, the fluorescence emission intensity and blue shift of the emission peak for 2-3-CDs became stronger. This is due to the fluorescence intensity significantly increasing with the rising concentration of Sc3+, indicating that more active sites in 2-3-CDs become inactive as the concentration increases. At low concentrations, the prevalence of active sites facilitates the coordination of 2-3-CDs with metal ions, resulting in stronger interactions. The detection of REEs also showed a similar phenomenon as reported in our previous study [27], with no change in emission peak wavelength but a stronger emission peak intensity as the metal concentration increases. Fig. 10(b) shows the linear relationship between Sc3+ ion concentration and relative fluorescence intensity (F/F
0 ). The calibration curve displayed a good linear relationship in the 2–20 μM (R2 = 0.9955). The linear regression equation was y = 0.1028x+0.9406, and LOD was 0.0974 μM (4.38 ppb). The above results indicated that 2-3-CDs have a higher detection limit than o-CDs, which improved the detection limit level of carbon dots as Sc3+ fluorescence sensors in fluorescence methods. The samples were tested with ICP-OES analysis to validate the sensor. The ICP-OES results further confirmed the presence of scandium. Comparing the Fluorescence results, which indicate stronger peak intensity with the concentration increase (Fig. 10(a)), the ICP-OES results also indicate higher Scandium presence with the increase in concentration (Fig. S12). Furthermore, the ICP-OES analysis indicates a concentration of 19.74 ppb in the real lake water sample.

The performance comparison of Sc3+ fluorescence sensors was summarized in Table 3 . Among all the mentioned Sc3+ detection methods, the LOD of this method does not seem particularly prominent, but it presents a significant advantage in analysis time. This work is the first to discover that carbon dots can serve as Sc3+ ion fluorescence sensors. Compared with organic ligands based on fluorescence detection of Sc3+ ions in the same field, the raw materials of carbon dots are readily available, and the preparation method is safe and fast, making the entire fluorescence detection process more economical and convenient. Therefore, the Sc3+ ion fluorescence sensor prepared by carbon dots in this work has practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference performance of Sc3+ detection by 2-3-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the anti-interference performance of 2-3-CDs in specific detecting Sc3+, interference ions (Y3+, La3+, Ce3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+) were added to the mixed solution of 2-3-CDs in the presence of Sc3+ (i.e., Blank referred to the mixed 2-3-CDs solution with only Sc3+ added). Fig. 11 (a–b) shows the fluorescence emission spectrum of 2-3-CDs solution, 2-3-CDs solution with only Sc3+ added (Blank), and various individual interfering ions and all other rare earth ions further added in the Blank. The interfering ions can marginally quench the fluorescence intensity of the 2-3-CDs-Sc3+ mixed solution (Blank), but the position of the fluorescence emission peak remains unaffected. The fluorescence phenomena exhibited by 2-3-CDs as sensors for detecting rare earth ions was attributed to the influence of Sc3+, with its fluorescence intensity enhancement factor marginally decreasing from 5.83 to 4.46 times in comparison to 2-3-CDs that exclusively detect Sc3+. The above results demonstrated that 2-3-CDs have excellent anti-interference performance as fluorescence sensors for Sc3+.

The Sc3+ solutions with different concentration gradients were prepared using real samples (tap water and lake water) and tested the performance of 2-3-CDs in actual scenarios. Fig. 12 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of (a) tap water and (c) lake water with different Sc3+ concentrations added to the 2-3-CDs solution. As the Sc3+ concentration increased, the fluorescence emission peaks of the 2-3-CDs-Sc3+ mixed solution gradually shifted to the left, increasing the fluorescence intensity. The standard curves for determining Sc3+ in tap water/lake water displayed a good linear relationship in the 5–30 μM range. The linear equations were F/F 0 = 0.0592CSc(Ш)+1.085, F/F 0 = 0.0621CSc(Ш)+1.0937, and the correlation coefficients R2 were 0.9989 and 0.9965, respectively. The use of tap water and lake water testing has demonstrated that 2-3-CDs have good anti-interference performance against various metal ions, organic compounds, and minerals present in real samples when detecting Sc3+. The detection effect of this fluorescence sensor can still maintain linear correlation, further manifesting the practical application prospects of 2-3-CDs.

Considering the excellent performance of 2-3-CDs in detecting Sc3+, where the fluorescence emission peak shifted blue from 510 nm to 470 nm, an attempt was made to prepare a luminescent thin film that can directly observe this phenomenon under ultraviolet light. By doping 2-3-CDs into PVA, the obtained thin film was uniformly transparent. Under 254 nm ultraviolet light irradiation, these thin films exhibited transparent green luminescence, as shown in Fig. 13 (a), proving that the optical properties of 2-3-CDs solution can be easily converted into solid 2-3-CDs polymer composite materials, which was beneficial for the practical application of 2-3-CDs. Different rare earth ion solutions were dripped onto the surface of the 2-3-CDs-PVA composite film. It was observed that only the 2-3-CDs-PVA-Sc3+ film changed from green luminescence to blue luminescence. In contrast, the colors of the other fifteen composite films did not show significant changes, as shown in Fig. 13(b). The advantages of using fluorescence spectrophotometry to detect rare earth ions lie in low instrument cost, simple operation, and the ability to design portable systems for on-site deployment. Developing a 2-3-CDs-PVA composite film that can specifically detect rare earth ions was very important for on-site qualitative detection. Only a UV lamp was needed to achieve qualitative detection; the operation was straightforward, and the detection time was also swift.

In this work, various carbon dots were synthesized via the “bottom-up” hydrothermal method under optimized preparation parameters. All achieved Sc3+ specific detection and exhibited excellent fluorescence stability; their intensity and peak position remained unchanged even after adding other interfering rare earth ions. The characteristic structure of “salicylic acid” was determined as the core of Sc3+ selective detection function through precursor molecular engineering design. o-CDs derived from salicylic acid achieved the highest fluorescence intensity and excellent anti-interference performance in Sc3+ fluorescence detection. The electronic and spatial effects of various precursors, as well as the influence of aromatic conjugated systems size on the Sc3+ specific detection performance was investigated, and the optimal 2-3-CDs derived from 2-hydroxy-3-naphthoic acid were selected, with a lower detection limit (4.38 ppb) and a significant blue shift (from green visible light to blue visible light range). A portable device capable of detecting Sc3+ ions, the 2-3-CDs PVA membrane, was developed with the optimal 2-3-CDs. Significant color changes (from green to blue) were observed in the existence of Sc3+ with a simple UV lamp irradiation, achieving specific and visual detection of Sc3+.

Zhe Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Heyu Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Aaron S. Pittman: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yan Cao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22178339), the Science and Technology Fundamental Project of Guangxi Province (23023021), and the University Science Research Project of Anhui Province (2022AH050076).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2025.02.356.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chu, X., S.N. Wang, and Y. Cao, A new fluorescence probe comprising nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for the selective and quantitative determination of cerium(iv). New Journal of Chemistry, 2020. 44(3): p. 797-806.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exploration of the synthesis of three types of multicolor carbon dot originating from isomers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, Y., Q. Su, and X. Yang, Exploration of the synthesis of three types of multicolor carbon dot originating from isomers. Chemical Communications, 2018. 54(80): p. 11312-11315.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, W., et al., Ethanothermal synthesis of phenol-derived carbon dots with multiple color emission via a versatile oxidation strategy. Optical Materials, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering triangular carbon quantum dots with unprecedented narrow bandwidth emission for multicolored LEDs
